title: Ethical Perception, Complexity, and the Need for Models
purpose: Introduce students to layered emergence, complexity, and the idea that ethical outcomes may exceed individual intentions.
minimal_route: Ask students what the pattern shows that individual intention alone does not explain.
Close with the distinction between blame, responsibility, and system architecture.
full_route: Run the Traditional Schelling preset.
Run an expanded Capability Lab version with repair or shared-space conditions.
Compare what changes when the system includes more than local preference.
Use the glossary to clarify simulation output versus moral conclusion.
public_prompts: What did the simulation make visible that an ordinary debate about individual choices might hide?
How can a large-scale pattern emerge even when no single agent intends the whole pattern?
What would count as a fair intervention in this system?
private_prompts: Suggested private exploration: Where have you seen a difference between what people intended and what a system produced?
Suggested private exploration: How does recognizing emergence change how you assign responsibility?
instructor_notes: Do not overload Chapter 1 with every EECSL case.
Use Schelling as the key doorway into complexity, not as a full theory of social life.
Emphasize that simulation results invite inquiry; they do not automatically settle ethical judgment.

title: Ethical Attention, PRF, and the Structure of Moral Perception
purpose: Help students see that ethical perception is structured by attention, assumptions, values, language, and interpretive frames.
minimal_route: Review PRF-adjacent terms, evidence-governance distinctions, and Teaching FHRI caution language.
Ask students to distinguish source facts, model outputs, interpretations, and private applications.
full_route: Open the Cross-Case Dashboard.
Choose one case card and ask what different observers might notice first.
Introduce the public/private boundary: public case analysis is not private confession.
Use a short lens audit as a discussion exercise.
public_prompts: What does an ethical lens help us notice that we might otherwise ignore?
Why should a public case page distinguish evidence, model output, interpretation, and teaching prompt?
How can two students view the same case differently without either being careless?
private_prompts: Suggested private exploration: What kinds of ethical details do you usually notice first?
Suggested private exploration: What kinds of ethical details might you tend to overlook?
instructor_notes: Keep student PRF work private unless students freely choose to share.
Use Chapter 2 to stabilize vocabulary before later simulations become more complex.
Avoid turning private prompts into required disclosure.

title: Evidence Policy, Warning, and Ethical Lenses
primary_tool: Capability Lab Challenger / Boisjoly run mode
purpose: Show how warning signals can be suppressed, normalized, overwritten, or institutionally ignored under pressure.
minimal_route: Ask what happened to the warning signal.
Use one ethical lens question: whose knowledge could be heard?
Close with the difference between private courage and institutional warning capacity.
full_route: Run Challenger first.
Open the 2008 Financial Crisis EECSL case.
Compare engineering warning suppression with financial rating normalization.
Apply the evidence-governance distinction: fact, model output, interpretation, hypothesis.
public_prompts: What makes a warning institutionally hearable or dismissible?
Why is warning not simply a matter of individual courage?
How does the 2008 case extend the Challenger pattern to a larger institutional system?
private_prompts: Suggested private exploration: Have you ever seen a warning ignored in a group or institution? What made it hard to hear?
Suggested private exploration: What would make it easier for you to speak or listen responsibly under pressure?
instructor_notes: Keep the Challenger story central; use 2008 as an extension, not a replacement.
Emphasize evidence-channel integrity and professional responsibility.
Avoid suggesting that students must disclose personal whistleblowing experiences.

title: Systems, Emergence, SAGE, and Responsibility
secondary_case: Higher Education IPS or 2008 Financial Crisis
purpose: Help students understand responsibility in systems where outcomes emerge from feedback, incentives, thresholds, and institutional design.
minimal_route: Ask what changed when heat, voice, trust, or repair changed.
Connect the result to SAGE: seeing, analyzing, generating, engaging.
full_route: Run Schelling Traditional versus Emergent Complexity view.
Run Coordination Under Heat using a stress or repair preset.
Open the Cross-Case Dashboard.
Ask students to identify one T1 repair and one T2 repair possibility.
public_prompts: When does responsibility belong partly to system architecture?
What changes when coordination is technically successful but ethically unhealthy?
How does SAGE help avoid jumping from first impression to final judgment?
private_prompts: Suggested private exploration: Where in your life do systems make good action easier or harder?
Suggested private exploration: How might SAGE help you slow down before judging a complex situation?
instructor_notes: Use Chapter 4 to connect critical thinking to systems thinking.
Make sure students do not equate coordination with goodness.
Use T1/T2 repair language carefully and point to the glossary.

title: Social Contract, Justice, and the Moral Commons
primary_tool: Civic coordination route with Repair preset
purpose: Connect Rawls, Nussbaum, John Hope Franklin, functional equivalence, Maathai-style shared task coordination, and the moral commons.
minimal_route: Introduce formal rights versus actual capabilities.
Use the civic coordination Repair preset.
Ask how different PRFs can support the same shared task for different authentic reasons.
Connect to John Hope Franklin as the emotional anchor.
full_route: Run civic coordination with Repair preset.
Open GEO Florida as the empirical justice case.
Use Higher Education IPS as a rights-to-capabilities extension.
Ask students to distinguish Rawlsian fairness, Nussbaum capabilities, and functional equivalence.
public_prompts: What is the difference between having a formal right and having the actual capability to use it?
How can different communities cooperate without sharing the same worldview?
What does the moral commons require if justice is more than formal permission?
private_prompts: Suggested private exploration: Where have you seen a gap between formal opportunity and actual capability?
Suggested private exploration: What shared task could different communities support for different authentic reasons?
instructor_notes: Treat care ethics as one framework among the chapter's tools, not the organizing center.
Keep Father Hanna out of Chapter 5 and reserve that case for later Smithian sympathy work.
Use Maathai / Green Belt as the secondary functional-equivalence case.
